title: Easy Sourdough Recipes for Camping
meta_description: Discover easy camping sourdough recipes that work with a wooden spurtle. Learn how to mix, bake, and enjoy fresh sourdough on the trail with simple gear and ste
html:
When you’re out in the woods, the smell of fresh sourdough baking over a campfire can feel like a five‑star breakfast. The trick is using the right tools—like a wooden spurtle—to keep your starter alive and your dough smooth, even in a backpacking kitchen. Below are three straightforward camping sourdough recipes, plus tips for handling starter, mixing dough, and cleaning up with a wooden spurtle.

Why a Wooden Spurtle Is the Best Camping Companion

A wooden spurtle (also called a Danish dough whisk) is slim, sturdy, and non‑scratch, making it perfect for stirring starter in a glass jar and scraping the sides clean. Unlike metal spoons, it won’t react with the acidity of a sourdough starter, and its natural wood feel helps you gauge dough consistency by touch. The 2 PCS Wooden Spurtle & Sourdough Starter Stirrer Set is especially handy because you get two sticks—one for mixing and one for jar scraping—so you can keep the starter and the dough separate without cross‑contamination.

Preparing Your Starter on the Trail

Before you head out, feed your starter a day or two in advance so it’s active and bubbly. Pack it in a clean, wide‑mouth glass jar with a loose‑fit lid (or a piece of breathable cloth secured with a rubber band). When you arrive at camp, give it a quick stir with the wooden spurtle to redistribute the yeast and bacteria.

  1. Remove the lid or cloth and stir the starter once with the spurtle.
  2. Feed 1 ½ cups flour and 1 ½ cups water, mixing until smooth.
  3. Cover loosely and let sit at room temperature for 4–6 hours, or until it doubles in size.

The spurtle’s tapered shape lets you reach the bottom of the jar easily, ensuring no dry pockets are left behind.

Campfire Sourdough Flatbread

This is the quickest recipe you can pull off with minimal equipment—just a skillet, a campfire, and your wooden spurtle.

  • Ingredients: 1 cup active starter, 1 cup all‑purpose flour, ¼ cup water, pinch of salt.
  • Method: In a metal bowl, combine starter, flour, water, and salt. Use the spurtle to whisk until a sticky dough forms—no need for a stand mixer.
  • Let the dough rest 30 minutes. While it rests, build a moderate fire and heat a cast‑iron skillet.
  • Drop spoonfuls of dough onto the skillet, flatten with the back of the spurtle, and cook 2‑3 minutes per side until golden spots appear.

These flatbreads are perfect for topping with cheese, smoked salmon, or just a drizzle of honey.

One‑Pan Sourdough Camp Loaf

If you have a Dutch oven or a heavy‑sided pot, you can bake a small loaf that holds up well for a day of hiking.

Ingredients: 2 cups active starter, 2 cups flour, ¾ cup water, 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp honey (optional).

Steps:

  1. Mix starter, flour, water, salt, and honey in a bowl using the wooden spurtle until a shaggy dough forms.
  2. Cover with a clean cloth and let rise 2‑3 hours, or until roughly doubled.
  3. Turn dough onto a lightly floured surface, shape into a round, and place in a pre‑heated Dutch oven.
  4. Cover and bake over coals for 25‑30 minutes, removing the lid for the last 5 minutes to develop a crust.

Because the spurtle is thin, you can scrape the dough from the bowl without tearing it, preserving the airy crumb.

Cleaning Up Without a Sink

One of the biggest challenges on a backcountry trip is keeping your gear clean. The wooden spurtle excels here, too. After mixing, simply use the spurtle to scrape any remaining dough from the jar, then rinse the jar with a little water and wipe the spurtle with a cloth. The natural wood repels moisture, so it dries quickly and won’t harbor bacteria.

FAQ

Can I use a metal spoon instead of a wooden spurtle?

You can, but metal can react with the acidity of the starter and may leave scratches in glass jars. A wooden spurtle is non‑reactive, gentle on jars, and gives you better control when shaping dough.

How many wooden spurtles should I bring?

The 2 PCS set is ideal for most trips—one for stirring starter and one for scraping jars. If you plan to bake multiple loaves, a third spare won’t hurt, but it’s rarely necessary.

Will the wood affect the flavor of my sourdough?

No. The wood is food‑safe and doesn’t leach flavors. It simply provides a tactile surface that helps you feel the dough’s development, which is especially useful when you can’t see the dough clearly in low‑light camp conditions.
